---
title: "ML Simplified"
description: "The math behind machine learning, explained four ways — every formula read as a plain-language table, each with an interactive demo. A static OneNote-style reference."
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "Topics"
toc-depth: 3
page-layout: full
include-in-header:
  - text: |
      <style>
      /* ===== OneNote notebook look ===== */
      #quarto-document-content { background: transparent; }
      .on-subject { margin-top: 1.2rem; }
      
      /* Section tabs (subjects) — coloured OneNote tabs */
      .panel-tabset.nav-pills > .nav { gap: .35rem; border-bottom: 2px solid rgba(0,0,0,.08); padding-bottom:.4rem; margin-bottom: 1rem; flex-wrap: wrap; }
      .panel-tabset.nav-pills > .nav .nav-link {
        border-radius: 8px 8px 0 0; font-weight: 600; color:#444; background:rgba(255,255,255,0.45); -webkit-backdrop-filter:blur(10px); backdrop-filter:blur(10px);
        padding:.45rem .9rem; border:1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.6); border-bottom:none;
      }
      .panel-tabset.nav-pills > .nav .nav-link.active { color:#fff; background:#4C4A98; }
      
      /* Topic tabs (pages) — left-rail feel via wrapping pills */
      .panel-tabset:not(.nav-pills) > .nav { gap:.3rem; flex-wrap:wrap; border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(0,0,0,.12); margin-bottom:1rem; }
      .panel-tabset:not(.nav-pills) > .nav .nav-link {
        border-radius:6px; font-size:.9rem; color:#4C4A98; background:rgba(76,74,152,.07);
        padding:.3rem .7rem; border:1px solid rgba(76,74,152,.15);
      }
      .panel-tabset:not(.nav-pills) > .nav .nav-link.active { color:#fff; background:#4C4A98; }
      
      /* Page card grid: math full width, then 3 explanation cards */
      .onenote-page { display:grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr; gap:1rem; margin:.5rem 0 1.5rem; }
      .onenote-page > .on-card { background:rgba(255,255,255,0.5); -webkit-backdrop-filter:blur(18px) saturate(160%); backdrop-filter:blur(18px) saturate(160%); border:1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.65); box-shadow:0 10px 32px rgba(76,74,152,0.12), inset 0 1px 0 rgba(255,255,255,0.7); border-radius:18px; padding:1rem 1.1rem; }
      .onenote-page > .on-math { grid-column: 1 / -1; border-left:5px solid #4C4A98; background:rgba(255,255,255,0.35); }
      .on-simple { border-left:5px solid #1f7a8c; }
      .on-ex     { border-left:5px solid #b8860b; }
      .on-real   { border-left:5px solid #9b3b6a; }
      @media (max-width: 900px){ .onenote-page { grid-template-columns:1fr; } }
      
      .on-label { font-size:.72rem; text-transform:uppercase; letter-spacing:.08em; font-weight:700; color:#777; margin-bottom:.5rem; }
      
      /* ===== Formula-as-table ===== */
      .frm-wrap { }
      .frm-formula {
        font-family:'JetBrains Mono', monospace; font-size:1.15rem; font-weight:600;
        background:rgba(31,31,44,0.88); -webkit-backdrop-filter:blur(8px); backdrop-filter:blur(8px); color:#e9e8ff; padding:.7rem 1rem; border-radius:8px;
        overflow-x:auto; white-space:nowrap; margin-bottom:.9rem;
      }
      .frm-frac { display:inline-block; text-align:center; margin:.2rem 0 1rem; font-family:'JetBrains Mono',monospace; }
      .frm-num, .frm-den { padding:.25rem .6rem; font-size:1rem; }
      .frm-bar { height:3px; background:#4C4A98; margin:.15rem 0; border-radius:2px; }
      .frm-table { width:100%; border-collapse:collapse; font-size:.92rem; margin-bottom:.8rem; }
      .frm-table th { text-align:left; background:#4C4A98; color:#fff; padding:.4rem .6rem; font-size:.8rem; }
      .frm-table td { border:1px solid rgba(0,0,0,.1); padding:.45rem .6rem; vertical-align:top; }
      .frm-table .frm-sym, .frm-table .frm-top, .frm-table .frm-bot { font-family:'JetBrains Mono',monospace; font-weight:600; color:#4C4A98; white-space:nowrap; }
      .frm-table .frm-sym span, .frm-table .frm-top span, .frm-table .frm-bot span { font-weight:600; }
      .frm-table .frm-top { color:#1f7a8c; }
      .frm-table .frm-bot { color:#9b3b6a; }
      .frm-reads { background:rgba(76,74,152,.06); border-radius:8px; padding:.55rem .8rem; font-size:.95rem; }
      
      /* Dark mode */
      body.quarto-dark .onenote-page > .on-card { background:rgba(34,33,52,0.55); border-color:rgba(182,180,240,0.18); box-shadow:0 10px 32px rgba(0,0,0,0.45), inset 0 1px 0 rgba(182,180,240,0.12); }
      body.quarto-dark .onenote-page > .on-math { background:rgba(30,29,46,0.45); }
      body.quarto-dark .frm-table td { border-color:rgba(255,255,255,.12); color:#d8d8e0; }
      body.quarto-dark .on-label { color:#a0a0b8; }
      body.quarto-dark .panel-tabset.nav-pills > .nav .nav-link { background:rgba(34,33,52,0.55); color:#cfcfe0; border-color:rgba(182,180,240,0.18); }
      body.quarto-dark .frm-reads { background:rgba(182,180,240,.1); }
      </style>
  - text: |
      <link rel="stylesheet" href="/styles/ml-viz.css">
include-after-body:
  - text: |
      <script src="/styles/ml-viz.js"></script>
---

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" icon=false}
### 📓 How to read this notebook

Pick a **subject tab**, then a **topic tab**. Each topic has four panes — and the formula is shown as a plain-language **table** (fractions become top-over-bottom). Every Mathematical pane also has a **🎮 live interactive demo** you can drag and play with.
:::

# Linear Algebra

::: {.on-subject}

::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills}

## Vectors

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Vector Addition

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">a + b = [ a₁+b₁ , a₂+b₂ , … , aₙ+bₙ ]</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">a , b</td><td>the two arrows (lists of numbers) you're combining</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">aᵢ + bᵢ</td><td>add the matching slots, one position at a time</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">result</td><td>a new arrow — the tip-to-tail diagonal of the two</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Add two arrows by adding their matching numbers; the answer is where you land if you walk the first arrow then the second.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="vecadd" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Stack two arrows tip-to-tail; where you end up is the sum. Order doesn't matter.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

[1,2] + [3,1] = [4,3].  Reference: 3Blue1Brown, *Essence of Linear Algebra* Ch.1.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Summing gradient contributions across a batch; moving an embedding by a 'direction' (king − man + woman ≈ queen).
:::

:::

### Vector Subtraction

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">a − b = [ a₁−b₁ , a₂−b₂ , … , aₙ−bₙ ]</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">a − b</td><td>subtract matching slots</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">result</td><td>the arrow pointing FROM b TO a</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Subtract matching numbers; the answer is the arrow that takes you from b to a — 'where you are minus where you were'.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="vecsub" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

The arrow that gets you from one point to another.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

[4,3] − [3,1] = [1,2].
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Error vectors (prediction − target); displacement between two GPS points; embedding differences that capture relationships.
:::

:::

### Dot Product

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">a · b = a₁b₁ + a₂b₂ + … + aₙbₙ = ‖a‖ ‖b‖ cos θ</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">a₁b₁ + a₂b₂ + …</td><td>multiply matching slots, then add them all up</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">‖a‖ ‖b‖</td><td>the two arrows' lengths multiplied</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">cos θ</td><td>how aligned they are: 1 = same way, 0 = perpendicular, −1 = opposite</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">result</td><td>one number: how much the two arrows point the same way</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Multiply-and-sum the matching numbers; the bigger the result, the more the two arrows agree in direction.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="dot" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

A single number measuring how much two vectors point the same way. Big = aligned, 0 = unrelated, negative = opposite.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

[1,2]·[3,1] = 1·3 + 2·1 = 5.  Cosine similarity is the normalised dot product.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Every neuron computes a dot product of weights and inputs; attention scores are query·key dot products; recommender similarity.
:::

:::

### Cross Product

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">a × b  ⟂ both,  ‖a × b‖ = ‖a‖ ‖b‖ sin θ</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">a × b</td><td>a NEW arrow at right angles to both inputs (3D only)</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">‖a‖ ‖b‖ sin θ</td><td>its length = area of the parallelogram a and b form</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">direction</td><td>perpendicular to the plane of a and b (right-hand rule)</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Combine two 3D arrows to get a third arrow that sticks straight out of the surface they span.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="cross" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Multiply two 3D arrows to get a third arrow at right angles to both — the 'normal' to their plane.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

[1,0,0] × [0,1,0] = [0,0,1].
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Surface normals in 3D graphics and robotics; torque and angular momentum in physics engines; camera orientation.
:::

:::

### L1 Norm

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">‖x‖₁ = |x₁| + |x₂| + … + |xₙ|</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">|xᵢ|</td><td>drop the minus signs — take each number's size</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">sum</td><td>add up all those sizes</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">result</td><td>total 'city-block' distance</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Add up the absolute sizes of every component — the distance if you can only travel along a grid.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="norm" data-params="{&quot;kind&quot;: &quot;l1&quot;}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Total distance if you can only move along grid streets, never diagonally.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

‖[3,−4]‖₁ = 3 + 4 = 7.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

L1 (Lasso) regularisation pushes weights to exactly zero → automatic feature selection; robust error metric (MAE).
:::

:::

### L2 Norm

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">‖x‖₂ = √( x₁² + x₂² + … + xₙ² )</div>
<div class="frm-frac">
  <div class="frm-num">√( x₁² + x₂² + … )</div>
  <div class="frm-bar"></div>
  <div class="frm-den">(under the root)</div>
</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Part</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-top">▲ Top<br><span>√( x₁² + x₂² + … )</span></td><td>square each number, add them, then take the square root</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-bot">▼ Bottom<br><span>(under the root)</span></td><td>this is just the Pythagorean theorem in n dimensions</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Square every component, add them, square-root the total — the straight-line length of the arrow.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="norm" data-params="{&quot;kind&quot;: &quot;l2&quot;}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

The 'as-the-crow-flies' length of the arrow.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

‖[3,−4]‖₂ = √(9+16) = √25 = 5.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

L2 / weight decay keeps weights small and smooth; normalising embeddings to unit length; gradient clipping by norm.
:::

:::

### Projection

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">projꞵ a = ( a · b / ‖b‖² ) · b</div>
<div class="frm-frac">
  <div class="frm-num">a · b</div>
  <div class="frm-bar"></div>
  <div class="frm-den">‖b‖²</div>
</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Part</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-top">▲ Top<br><span>a · b</span></td><td>how much a points along b (their dot product)</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-bot">▼ Bottom<br><span>‖b‖²</span></td><td>b's length squared — divides out b's size so it's a fair ratio</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Divide 'how much a leans toward b' by 'b's size', then scale b by that — the shadow a casts on b.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="proj" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

The shadow one arrow casts on another when light shines straight down on it.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Projecting [2,2] onto the x-axis [1,0] gives [2,0].
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

PCA projects data onto principal directions; least-squares regression projects targets onto the feature space.
:::

:::

### Orthogonality

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">a ⟂ b   ⟺   a · b = 0</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">a · b = 0</td><td>their dot product is exactly zero</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">⟂</td><td>the arrows meet at 90° — perpendicular</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">meaning</td><td>they share no common direction at all</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> If the dot product is zero, the two arrows are at right angles and completely unrelated in direction.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="ortho" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Two directions that share no overlap — knowing one tells you nothing about the other.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

[1,0]·[0,1] = 0 → the axes are orthogonal.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Orthogonal weight init prevents signal blow-up; orthogonal bases (Fourier, wavelets) for compression; decorrelated features.
:::

:::

:::

## Matrices

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Matrix Multiplication

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">(AB)ᵢⱼ = Aᵢ₁B₁ⱼ + Aᵢ₂B₂ⱼ + … + AᵢₙBₙⱼ</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">row i of A</td><td>one horizontal strip from the first matrix</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">column j of B</td><td>one vertical strip from the second matrix</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">dot product</td><td>multiply them slot-by-slot and sum → cell (i,j) of the answer</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">rule</td><td>inner sizes must match: (m×n)(n×p) = (m×p)</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Each output cell is the dot product of a row from A and a column from B; it chains two transformations into one.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="lintransform" data-params="{&quot;mode&quot;: &quot;matmul&quot;}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Every output cell is a row·column dot product. It glues two linear transformations together.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

[[1,2],[3,4]] · [1,1]ᵀ = [3,7]ᵀ.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

The heart of deep learning — every dense layer is Wx+b; ~99% of training compute is matrix multiplication (why GPUs exist).
:::

:::

### Transpose

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">(Aᵀ)ᵢⱼ = Aⱼᵢ      (AB)ᵀ = Bᵀ Aᵀ</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">Aⱼᵢ</td><td>swap the row and column index of every entry</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">effect</td><td>rows become columns, columns become rows (flip across the diagonal)</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Flip the matrix across its diagonal so rows turn into columns.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="transpose" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Turn rows into columns and columns into rows.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

[[1,2],[3,4]]ᵀ = [[1,3],[2,4]].
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Backprop multiplies by Wᵀ to push gradients backward; Gram matrices XᵀX for covariance and style transfer.
:::

:::

### Inverse

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">A⁻¹ A = I      (exists if det A ≠ 0)</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">A⁻¹</td><td>the 'undo' matrix</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">A⁻¹A = I</td><td>applying it after A returns you to where you started (identity)</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">det A ≠ 0</td><td>only invertible if the matrix doesn't squash space flat</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> The inverse is the matrix that undoes A; it only exists when A doesn't collapse any dimension.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="lintransform" data-params="{&quot;mode&quot;: &quot;matmul&quot;}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

The 'undo' matrix — apply it to reverse what A did.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

[[2,0],[0,2]]⁻¹ = [[0.5,0],[0,0.5]].
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Closed-form regression β̂ = (XᵀX)⁻¹Xᵀy; Kalman filters; in practice we solve systems rather than invert (faster, stabler).
:::

:::

### Determinant

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">det [[a,b],[c,d]] = a·d − b·c</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">a·d</td><td>product of the main diagonal</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">b·c</td><td>product of the off-diagonal</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">difference</td><td>the signed area/volume scaling factor; 0 = space squashed flat</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Multiply the diagonals and subtract; the result tells you how much the matrix stretches area (zero = it flattens space).</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="lintransform" data-params="{&quot;mode&quot;: &quot;determinant&quot;}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

How much the transformation stretches or shrinks space. Zero means it squashes everything flat.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

det [[1,2],[3,4]] = 1·4 − 2·3 = −2.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Normalising flows use log|det J|; checking invertibility; computing Gaussian densities.
:::

:::

### Rank

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">rank(A) = number of independent rows (or columns)</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">independent rows</td><td>rows that can't be built from the others</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">count</td><td>how many genuinely different directions A can reach</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">low rank</td><td>lots of redundancy — the matrix is 'simpler' than its size</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Count how many rows are truly different; that's how many independent directions the matrix spans.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="lintransform" data-params="{&quot;mode&quot;: &quot;rank&quot;, &quot;m&quot;: [1, 0.5, 2, 1]}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

How many genuinely different directions the matrix can reach. Low rank = redundancy.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

[[1,2],[2,4]] has rank 1 (row 2 = 2 × row 1).
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Low-rank factorisation (LoRA!) fine-tunes LLMs with tiny matrices; recommenders factor the ratings matrix; PCA.
:::

:::

### Trace

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">tr(A) = A₁₁ + A₂₂ + … + Aₙₙ = Σ eigenvalues</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">Aᵢᵢ</td><td>the diagonal entries</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">sum</td><td>just add them up</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">= Σ eigenvalues</td><td>it also equals the sum of all eigenvalues</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Add up the diagonal entries — which happens to equal the sum of the eigenvalues.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="trace" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Just add up the diagonal entries.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

tr [[1,2],[3,4]] = 1 + 4 = 5.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Simplifies gradient derivations (cyclic identities); trace of covariance = total variance; information measures.
:::

:::

:::

## Core Concepts

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Linear Independence

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">c₁v₁ + c₂v₂ + … + cₙvₙ = 0  only when all cᵢ = 0</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">Σ cᵢvᵢ = 0</td><td>a weighted combination of the vectors equals the zero vector</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">all cᵢ = 0</td><td>the ONLY way to get zero is to use no vectors at all</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">meaning</td><td>none of the vectors is redundant</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> If the only way to combine the vectors into zero is to multiply them all by zero, none is redundant — they're independent.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="lintransform" data-params="{&quot;mode&quot;: &quot;rank&quot;}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

No vector in the set can be built from the others — none is wasted.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

[1,0] and [0,1] are independent; [1,0] and [2,0] are not.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Independent features avoid multicollinearity (unstable coefficients); guarantee a unique least-squares solution.
:::

:::

### Basis

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">any v = c₁e₁ + c₂e₂ + … + cₙeₙ   (unique cᵢ)</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">e₁ … eₙ</td><td>the building-block directions (independent + spanning)</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">cᵢ</td><td>the unique coordinates of v in that basis</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">any v</td><td>every vector in the space can be built this way</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> A basis is the smallest set of directions from which every vector can be uniquely rebuilt.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="lintransform" data-params="{&quot;mode&quot;: &quot;matmul&quot;, &quot;m&quot;: [1, 0, 0, 1]}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

The smallest set of building-block directions you can construct everything else from.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Standard basis of 3D space: [1,0,0], [0,1,0], [0,0,1].
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Embedding dimensions are a learned basis for meaning; Fourier/wavelet bases for compression; good basis = good features.
:::

:::

### Subspaces

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">closed:  u,v ∈ S  ⟹  u+v ∈ S  and  c·u ∈ S</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">u+v ∈ S</td><td>add two members → still inside</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">c·u ∈ S</td><td>scale a member → still inside</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">always contains 0</td><td>every subspace passes through the origin</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> A subspace is a flat slice (line or plane through the origin) that you can't escape by adding or scaling its members.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="lintransform" data-params="{&quot;mode&quot;: &quot;rank&quot;, &quot;m&quot;: [1, 0.5, 2, 1]}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

A flat 'slice' of space — a line or plane through the origin — that stays inside itself.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

The x–y plane is a 2D subspace of 3D space.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Data often lives on a low-dimensional subspace (manifold) inside high-dim space — the basis of dimensionality reduction.
:::

:::

### Eigenvalues

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">A v = λ v      found via  det(A − λI) = 0</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">A v</td><td>apply the matrix to a special vector v</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">λ v</td><td>the result is just v scaled by a number λ (no rotation)</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">λ</td><td>the eigenvalue — the stretch factor along that direction</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> An eigenvalue λ is the amount a matrix stretches one of its special, un-rotated directions.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="lintransform" data-params="{&quot;mode&quot;: &quot;eigen&quot;, &quot;m&quot;: [2, 0, 0, 1]}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

The scaling factors along the special directions a matrix doesn't rotate.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

[[2,0],[0,3]] has eigenvalues 2 and 3.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

PCA's eigenvalues = variance per component; PageRank is the top eigenvector; spectral norm bounds training stability.
:::

:::

### Eigenvectors

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">A v = λ v ,   v ≠ 0</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">v</td><td>a direction the matrix only stretches, never turns</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">λ</td><td>how much it stretches that direction</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">A v = λ v</td><td>applying A keeps v on the same line</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> An eigenvector is a direction that a transformation merely stretches without turning.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="lintransform" data-params="{&quot;mode&quot;: &quot;eigen&quot;, &quot;m&quot;: [2, 1, 1, 2]}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

The directions a transformation only stretches without rotating.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

For [[2,0],[0,3]], the eigenvectors are [1,0] and [0,1].
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Principal components are eigenvectors of the covariance matrix; eigenfaces for face recognition; vibration modes.
:::

:::

### Spectral Decomposition

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">A = Q Λ Qᵀ   (A symmetric)</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">Q</td><td>rotation: the orthonormal eigenvectors</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">Λ</td><td>scaling: a diagonal of eigenvalues</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">Qᵀ</td><td>rotate back</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> A symmetric matrix can be rewritten as: rotate → stretch each axis → rotate back.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="lintransform" data-params="{&quot;mode&quot;: &quot;eigen&quot;, &quot;m&quot;: [2, 1, 1, 2]}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Rewrite a symmetric matrix as rotate, scale each axis, then rotate back.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Covariance matrices are symmetric, so they always decompose this way.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Whitening / decorrelating data; matrix square-roots and powers; the math behind PCA and Gaussian processes.
:::

:::

### SVD

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">A = U Σ Vᵀ   (works for ANY matrix)</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">V ᵀ</td><td>first rotation (in the input space)</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">Σ</td><td>stretch along axes by the singular values</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">U</td><td>second rotation (in the output space)</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Any matrix at all is: rotate → stretch → rotate. The stretch amounts are the singular values.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="lintransform" data-params="{&quot;mode&quot;: &quot;matmul&quot;, &quot;m&quot;: [1.5, 0.8, 0, 1]}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

The universal decomposition: rotate, stretch along axes, rotate again — for ANY matrix.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Keeping the top-k singular values gives the best rank-k approximation (Eckart–Young).
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Latent semantic analysis, image compression, Netflix-prize recommenders, and the theory behind low-rank LLM adapters.
:::

:::

### QR Decomposition

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">A = Q R</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">Q</td><td>orthonormal columns — a clean rotation part</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">R</td><td>upper-triangular — the 'bookkeeping' part</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">use</td><td>solve A x = b stably without forming A⁻¹</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Split a matrix into a clean rotation (Q) and a triangular remainder (R).</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="lintransform" data-params="{&quot;mode&quot;: &quot;matmul&quot;, &quot;m&quot;: [1, 1, 0, 1]}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Split a matrix into a clean rotation part and a triangular bookkeeping part.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Used to solve A x = b stably; the QR algorithm finds eigenvalues.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Numerically stable least-squares; eigenvalue computation; orthogonalising layers in deep nets.
:::

:::

### Positive Definite Matrices

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">xᵀ A x > 0   for all x ≠ 0   ⟺   all eigenvalues > 0</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">xᵀ A x</td><td>a quadratic 'energy' score for any input x</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">> 0</td><td>always positive — the surface curves upward everywhere</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">eigenvalues > 0</td><td>equivalent condition: every stretch factor is positive</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> If the matrix gives a positive 'energy' for every input, it's a perfect bowl with one lowest point.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="convexsurface" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

A matrix that always curves upward — like a perfect bowl with a single lowest point.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

The identity matrix is positive definite; any covariance matrix is at least semi-definite.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Guarantees a unique optimum in convex problems; enables Cholesky factorisation; kernel matrices in SVMs and GPs.
:::

:::

### Tensor Algebra

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">shape (batch, height, width, channels) — n axes</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">scalar</td><td>0 axes — a single number</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">vector / matrix</td><td>1 / 2 axes</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">tensor</td><td>3+ axes — e.g. a batch of colour images</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">contraction</td><td>the einsum generalisation of matrix multiply</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Tensors are arrays with any number of axes; deep-learning data and weights are tensors.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="matnote" data-params="{&quot;text&quot;: &quot;A batch of 32 RGB 224\u00d7224 images is a 4-D tensor: shape (32, 224, 224, 3). Each axis is a dimension you index into.&quot;}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Arrays with more than two axes — e.g. a batch of colour images is a 4D tensor.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

A 224×224 RGB batch of 32 images has shape (32, 224, 224, 3).
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Every DL framework (PyTorch, TensorFlow) is a tensor engine; attention runs on 4D tensors; einsum expresses contractions.
:::

:::

:::

:::

:::

# Calculus

::: {.on-subject}

::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills}

## Foundations

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Limits

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">lim (x→a) f(x) = L</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">x → a</td><td>let the input creep toward a</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">f(x) → L</td><td>the output settles toward L</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">meaning</td><td>the value the function heads to, even if it never arrives</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> As the input approaches a, the output approaches L — the value the function is heading toward.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="limit" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

What value a function is heading toward as the input approaches a point.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

lim (x→0) sin(x)/x = 1.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Underpins why gradient-descent steps work; learning-rate decay 'in the limit'; defining derivatives that power backprop.
:::

:::

### Derivatives

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">f′(x) = lim (h→0)  [ f(x+h) − f(x) ] / h</div>
<div class="frm-frac">
  <div class="frm-num">f(x+h) − f(x)</div>
  <div class="frm-bar"></div>
  <div class="frm-den">h</div>
</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Part</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-top">▲ Top<br><span>f(x+h) − f(x)</span></td><td>how much the output changes over a tiny step h</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-bot">▼ Bottom<br><span>h</span></td><td>the size of that tiny step (shrunk toward zero)</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Change in output divided by a vanishingly small change in input — the slope right at this point.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="derivative" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

How fast the output changes when you nudge the input — the steepness right here.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

d/dx (x²) = 2x; at x=3 the slope is 6.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

The gradient (vector of derivatives) tells each weight which way and how much to move to cut the loss.
:::

:::

### Partial Derivatives

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">∂f/∂xᵢ  =  slope in the xᵢ direction (others fixed)</div>
<div class="frm-frac">
  <div class="frm-num">∂f</div>
  <div class="frm-bar"></div>
  <div class="frm-den">∂xᵢ</div>
</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Part</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-top">▲ Top<br><span>∂f</span></td><td>tiny change in the output</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-bot">▼ Bottom<br><span>∂xᵢ</span></td><td>tiny change in ONE input, holding all the rest frozen</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> The output's slope when you wiggle just one input and freeze every other.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="gradientfield" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Change in the output when you wiggle one input and freeze the rest.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

f = x²y → ∂f/∂x = 2xy, ∂f/∂y = x².
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Nets have millions of parameters; each weight update uses the partial derivative of the loss w.r.t. that weight.
:::

:::

### Chain Rule

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">d/dx f(g(x)) = f′(g(x)) · g′(x)</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">g′(x)</td><td>slope of the inner function</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">f′(g(x))</td><td>slope of the outer function, measured at g(x)</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">multiply</td><td>chain the slopes together, outside-in</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> To differentiate nested functions, multiply the outer slope by the inner slope.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="derivative" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

For nested functions, multiply the slopes of each layer together, outside-in.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

d/dx sin(x²) = cos(x²) · 2x.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Backpropagation IS the chain rule applied layer by layer — the single most important formula in deep learning.
:::

:::

### Gradients

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">∇f = [ ∂f/∂x₁ , ∂f/∂x₂ , … , ∂f/∂xₙ ]</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">each ∂f/∂xᵢ</td><td>the slope in one input direction</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">vector</td><td>bundle all the slopes into one arrow</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">∇f</td><td>points toward the steepest increase</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Bundle every partial slope into one arrow; it points uphill the fastest — so step the other way to go down.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="gradientfield" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

An arrow pointing toward the fastest increase; step opposite to go downhill.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

f = x² + y² → ∇f = [2x, 2y].
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Gradient descent moves parameters along −∇Loss; the whole training loop is 'compute gradient, take a step'.
:::

:::

### Jacobian

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">Jᵢⱼ = ∂fᵢ/∂xⱼ      (matrix of all partials)</div>
<div class="frm-frac">
  <div class="frm-num">∂fᵢ</div>
  <div class="frm-bar"></div>
  <div class="frm-den">∂xⱼ</div>
</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Part</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-top">▲ Top<br><span>∂fᵢ</span></td><td>change in output number i</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-bot">▼ Bottom<br><span>∂xⱼ</span></td><td>change in input number j — filled in for every (i,j) pair</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> A full table of how every output reacts to every input — the gradient generalised to many outputs.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="gradientfield" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

A table of how every output reacts to every input.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

f(x,y) = [x², xy] → J = [[2x, 0],[y, x]].
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Normalising flows use log|det J|; sensitivity analysis; vector-Jacobian products power autodiff backprop.
:::

:::

### Hessian

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">Hᵢⱼ = ∂²f / ∂xᵢ ∂xⱼ   (curvature)</div>
<div class="frm-frac">
  <div class="frm-num">∂²f</div>
  <div class="frm-bar"></div>
  <div class="frm-den">∂xᵢ ∂xⱼ</div>
</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Part</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-top">▲ Top<br><span>∂²f</span></td><td>the SECOND change in the output</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-bot">▼ Bottom<br><span>∂xᵢ ∂xⱼ</span></td><td>with respect to a pair of inputs — how the slope itself bends</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> The second-derivative table: tells you whether you're in a bowl, a dome, or a saddle.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="convexsurface" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Tells you how the slope itself is changing — bowl, dome, or saddle.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

f = x² + y² → H = [[2,0],[0,2]] (positive definite → a bowl).
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Second-order optimisers (Newton, L-BFGS) use it; its eigenvalues diagnose saddle points that slow training.
:::

:::

### Taylor Series

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">f(x) ≈ f(a) + f′(a)(x−a) + ½ f″(a)(x−a)² + …</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">f(a)</td><td>start from the value at a</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">f′(a)(x−a)</td><td>add the slope term (linear correction)</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">½ f″(a)(x−a)²</td><td>add the curvature term (quadratic correction)</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Approximate any smooth curve near a point using its value, slope, curvature, and so on.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="taylor" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Approximate any smooth curve near a point using a polynomial built from its derivatives.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

eˣ ≈ 1 + x + x²/2 near 0.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Justifies gradient descent (first-order) and Newton's method (second-order); solver approximations.
:::

:::

### Integration

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx = signed area under the curve</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">f(x) dx</td><td>a thin slice: height × tiny width</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">∫ₐᵇ</td><td>add up infinitely many slices from a to b</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">result</td><td>total accumulated area / quantity / probability</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Add up infinitely many thin slices under the curve to get a total.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="integral" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Add up infinitely many thin slices to get a total — area, accumulated quantity, or probability.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

∫₀¹ x dx = ½.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Expected values and probabilities are integrals; the ELBO in VAEs; diffusion models integrate an SDE to generate images.
:::

:::

### Numerical Differentiation

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">f′(x) ≈ [ f(x+h) − f(x−h) ] / (2h)</div>
<div class="frm-frac">
  <div class="frm-num">f(x+h) − f(x−h)</div>
  <div class="frm-bar"></div>
  <div class="frm-den">2h</div>
</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Part</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-top">▲ Top<br><span>f(x+h) − f(x−h)</span></td><td>measure the function a little ahead and a little behind</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-bot">▼ Bottom<br><span>2h</span></td><td>divide by the total gap between those two points</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Estimate a slope by sampling the function just ahead and just behind, then dividing by the gap.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="derivative" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Estimate a slope by measuring the function at nearby points — no formula needed.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

With h=0.01, central difference of x² at 3 ≈ 6.00.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Gradient-checking to verify hand-written backprop; finite-difference sensitivities when analytic gradients are missing.
:::

:::

### Numerical Optimization

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">θ ← θ − (step) · (search direction),  repeat</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">start θ</td><td>an initial guess</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">search direction</td><td>which way improves the objective (e.g. −gradient)</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">repeat</td><td>keep stepping until it stops improving</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> When you can't solve for the best answer directly, search for it step by step, improving each round.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="graddesc" data-params="{&quot;surface&quot;: &quot;convex&quot;}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Search for the best answer step by step, improving each iteration.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Minimising a non-linear least-squares loss with Levenberg–Marquardt.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Training every neural network; calibrating physics models; portfolio optimisation; hyperparameter search.
:::

:::

:::

:::

:::

# Optimization

::: {.on-subject}

::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills}

## Objectives & Losses

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Objective Functions

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">minimise (or maximise)  f(θ)  over parameters θ</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">θ</td><td>the knobs the model can change</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">f(θ)</td><td>a single score measuring how good those settings are</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">min / max</td><td>push that score the best direction</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> A single 'goodness' score the whole search tries to make as good as possible.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="graddesc" data-params="{&quot;surface&quot;: &quot;convex&quot;}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

The single score you're trying to make as good as possible.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Maximise reward in RL; minimise prediction error in supervised learning.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Defines what 'good' means — choosing it wrong (clicks not satisfaction) causes misaligned systems.
:::

:::

### Loss Functions

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">MSE = (1/n) Σ (y − ŷ)²      CE = − Σ y log ŷ</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">y</td><td>the true answer</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">ŷ</td><td>the model's prediction</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">(y−ŷ)² or y log ŷ</td><td>penalty that grows as the prediction gets worse</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">average</td><td>mean penalty over all examples</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> A number that's big when the model is wrong and small when it's right; training drives it down.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="losscurves" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

A number that's big when the model is wrong and small when it's right.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Cross-entropy for classification; MSE for regression; contrastive loss for embeddings.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Loss choice shapes behaviour: MSE punishes outliers hard; cross-entropy suits probabilities; focal loss for imbalance.
:::

:::

### Convex Optimization

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">any local minimum  =  the global minimum</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">convex</td><td>the surface is a single smooth valley (f″ ≥ 0)</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">local min</td><td>wherever you stop rolling downhill</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">= global min</td><td>that stop is guaranteed to be THE best point</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> On a convex (bowl-shaped) surface, any bottom you reach is the one true bottom.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="convexsurface" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

A single smooth valley — wherever you roll downhill you reach the one true bottom.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Linear/logistic regression and SVMs are convex.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Convex problems solve reliably and provably — used in finance, control, and as the well-behaved core of larger systems.
:::

:::

### Non-Convex Optimization

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">many local minima, saddles, plateaus — no global guarantee</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">local minima</td><td>many 'good enough' valleys</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">saddle points</td><td>flat spots that aren't minima</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">no guarantee</td><td>you might not find the single deepest valley</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> A bumpy landscape of many dips; you may settle in a good valley, not necessarily the deepest.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="convexsurface" data-params="{&quot;nonconvex&quot;: true}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

A bumpy mountain range full of dips — you might settle in a 'good enough' valley.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Every deep neural network loss surface is non-convex.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Deep learning works anyway: in high dimensions most local minima are nearly as good, and SGD noise escapes bad spots.
:::

:::

:::

## Gradient Descent

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Batch Gradient Descent

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">θ ← θ − η · ∇Loss(whole dataset)</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">∇Loss(all data)</td><td>average advice from every single example</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">η</td><td>the learning rate (step size)</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">θ ← θ − …</td><td>step downhill once per full pass</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Look at every example, average their advice, then take one careful step downhill.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="graddesc" data-params="{&quot;surface&quot;: &quot;convex&quot;}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Look at every example, average the advice, then take one careful step. Accurate but slow.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Used when datasets fit in memory and a smooth path matters.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Rare for large data (one step = a full pass); used in classical ML and as a baseline.
:::

:::

### SGD

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">θ ← θ − η · ∇Loss(one random example)</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">one example</td><td>a single randomly-picked data point</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">∇Loss</td><td>its noisy gradient</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">step</td><td>update after every single example</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Step after every single example — noisy and jittery, but fast and able to escape traps.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="graddesc" data-params="{&quot;surface&quot;: &quot;convex&quot;, &quot;noise&quot;: 1.4}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Take a step after every example — noisy but fast, and able to escape traps.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Robbins–Monro stochastic approximation (1951).
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

The noise acts as a regulariser; foundation of modern training, usually replaced by mini-batches in practice.
:::

:::

### Mini-Batch SGD

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">θ ← θ − η · (1/B) Σ ∇Loss(batch of B)</div>
<div class="frm-frac">
  <div class="frm-num">Σ ∇Loss over B examples</div>
  <div class="frm-bar"></div>
  <div class="frm-den">B</div>
</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Part</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-top">▲ Top<br><span>Σ ∇Loss over B examples</span></td><td>add up the gradients of a small handful of examples</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-bot">▼ Bottom<br><span>B</span></td><td>divide by the batch size to average them</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Average the gradient over a small batch (e.g. 256), then step — stable enough and GPU-friendly.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="graddesc" data-params="{&quot;surface&quot;: &quot;convex&quot;, &quot;noise&quot;: 0.6}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Look at a small handful each step — stable enough, and perfect for GPU parallelism.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Batch size 256 is a common default.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

The actual workhorse of deep learning; batch size trades gradient noise vs hardware throughput.
:::

:::

:::

## Optimizers

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Momentum

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">v ← β v + ∇Loss ;   θ ← θ − η v   (β ≈ 0.9)</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">β v</td><td>keep 90% of last step's velocity</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">+ ∇Loss</td><td>add the new gradient</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">θ ← θ − η v</td><td>move along the built-up velocity</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Build up speed downhill like a rolling ball, pushing through small bumps and flat spots.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="graddesc" data-params="{&quot;surface&quot;: &quot;nonconvex&quot;, &quot;momentum&quot;: true}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Build up speed downhill like a rolling ball — push through bumps and flat spots.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Polyak's heavy-ball method.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Speeds convergence in ravines; SGD+momentum often beats fancier optimisers for vision.
:::

:::

### Nesterov Momentum

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">gradient measured at  θ − η β v  (look ahead)</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">θ − ηβv</td><td>peek where momentum is about to carry you</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">gradient there</td><td>measure the slope at that future spot</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">correct early</td><td>adjust before overshooting</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Peek where momentum is about to take you, measure the slope there, and correct early.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="graddesc" data-params="{&quot;surface&quot;: &quot;nonconvex&quot;, &quot;momentum&quot;: true}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Peek where momentum is about to carry you and correct early — an anticipatory ball.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Nesterov accelerated gradient (1983).
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Slightly faster and more stable than plain momentum; a flag in every framework.
:::

:::

### AdaGrad

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">step ∝ 1 / √( Σ past gradients² )</div>
<div class="frm-frac">
  <div class="frm-num">learning rate</div>
  <div class="frm-bar"></div>
  <div class="frm-den">√(Σ g²)</div>
</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Part</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-top">▲ Top<br><span>learning rate</span></td><td>the base step size</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-bot">▼ Bottom<br><span>√(Σ g²)</span></td><td>grows with accumulated gradient size → shrinks the step per parameter</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Give rarely-updated parameters big steps and frequently-updated ones small steps.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="graddesc" data-params="{&quot;surface&quot;: &quot;convex&quot;}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Big steps for rare parameters, small steps for frequent ones.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Great for sparse features (text, recommendations).
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Strong early, but the ever-growing denominator eventually kills the step — which motivated RMSProp/Adam.
:::

:::

### RMSProp

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">E[g²] ← γ E[g²] + (1−γ) g² ;   step ∝ 1/√E[g²]</div>
<div class="frm-frac">
  <div class="frm-num">learning rate</div>
  <div class="frm-bar"></div>
  <div class="frm-den">√E[g²]</div>
</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Part</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-top">▲ Top<br><span>learning rate</span></td><td>base step size</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-bot">▼ Bottom<br><span>√E[g²]</span></td><td>root of a DECAYING average of squared gradients — forgets old ones</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Like AdaGrad but forgets old gradients, so the step size never dies out.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="graddesc" data-params="{&quot;surface&quot;: &quot;convex&quot;}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Like AdaGrad but forgets old gradients, so the step doesn't die out.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Proposed by Hinton in a Coursera lecture.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Default for RNNs and reinforcement learning; handles non-stationary objectives well.
:::

:::

### Adam

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">θ ← θ − η · m̂ / ( √v̂ + ε )</div>
<div class="frm-frac">
  <div class="frm-num">m̂  (momentum)</div>
  <div class="frm-bar"></div>
  <div class="frm-den">√v̂ + ε</div>
</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Part</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-top">▲ Top<br><span>m̂  (momentum)</span></td><td>a decaying average of gradients — the direction with inertia</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-bot">▼ Bottom<br><span>√v̂ + ε</span></td><td>a decaying average of squared gradients — adapts the step size; ε avoids ÷0</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Combine momentum (direction) and RMSProp (adaptive size) into one robust update.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="graddesc" data-params="{&quot;surface&quot;: &quot;nonconvex&quot;, &quot;momentum&quot;: true}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Adaptive step size AND momentum together — robust defaults that 'just work'.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Kingma & Ba 2014; defaults β₁=0.9, β₂=0.999, η=1e-3.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

The most widely used optimiser in deep learning; trains transformers, GANs, and most published models.
:::

:::

### AdamW

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">Adam step  +  separate  λθ  weight shrink</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">Adam step</td><td>the usual adaptive momentum update</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">+ λθ</td><td>pull every weight toward zero, applied directly (decoupled)</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">decoupled</td><td>the shrink is NOT folded into the gradient — so it works correctly</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Adam, but the 'keep weights small' penalty is kept separate so it actually does its job.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="graddesc" data-params="{&quot;surface&quot;: &quot;nonconvex&quot;, &quot;momentum&quot;: true}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Adam done right — keeps the weight-shrinking penalty separate so it works as intended.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Loshchilov & Hutter 2017.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

The standard optimiser for training LLMs (BERT, GPT, LLaMA) — better generalisation than vanilla Adam.
:::

:::

### Learning Rate Scheduling

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">ηₜ = ½ η₀ ( 1 + cos(π t / T) )   (cosine decay)</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">η₀</td><td>the starting learning rate</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">cos(π t/T)</td><td>smoothly falls from +1 to −1 over training</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">ηₜ</td><td>so the rate eases from η₀ down to 0</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Start cautious, speed up, then slow down to settle — like easing off the gas near your destination.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="lrcurve" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Start cautious, go fast, then slow down to settle.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Warmup + cosine decay is standard for transformers.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Critical for LLM training stability; warmup prevents early divergence; cosine decay squeezes out final accuracy.
:::

:::

### Weight Decay

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">Loss  +  (λ/2) ‖θ‖²</div>
<div class="frm-frac">
  <div class="frm-num">(λ/2) ‖θ‖²</div>
  <div class="frm-bar"></div>
  <div class="frm-den">added to Loss</div>
</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Part</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-top">▲ Top<br><span>(λ/2) ‖θ‖²</span></td><td>a penalty that grows with the size of the weights</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-bot">▼ Bottom<br><span>added to Loss</span></td><td>so training is pushed to keep weights small AND fit the data</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Add a penalty for big weights so the model stays simple and generalises.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="convexsurface" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Gently pull every weight toward zero so the model stays simple.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

λ = 0.01 is a common value.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Reduces overfitting in nearly every trained model; in AdamW it's the main regulariser for LLMs.
:::

:::

### Early Stopping

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">stop when validation loss hasn't improved for p epochs</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">validation loss</td><td>error on held-out data</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">patience p</td><td>how many stalled epochs to tolerate</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">stop</td><td>quit before the model starts memorising</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Quit while you're ahead — stop once held-out error stops improving.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="traincurve" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Quit while you're ahead — stop before the model memorises the training set.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Patience of 5–10 epochs is typical.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Cheap, effective overfitting guard; saves compute; used everywhere from Kaggle to production.
:::

:::

:::

## Tuning

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Hyperparameter Optimization

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">search over { LR, depth, batch size, … } for best result</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">hyperparameters</td><td>settings you choose, not learned by gradient</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">search</td><td>grid / random / model-based exploration</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">best result</td><td>the config with the best validation score</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
```
