```{=html}
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Tune the hand-set knobs (learning rate, depth, …) to find the best-performing model.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="search" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Tune the 'knobs' you set by hand to get the best model.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Random search often beats grid search (Bergstra & Bengio 2012).
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Optuna / Ray Tune in practice; can swing accuracy by points — the gap between mediocre and winning.
:::

:::

### Bayesian Optimization

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">surrogate model  +  acquisition(next trial)</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">surrogate</td><td>a cheap model guessing how each config performs</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">acquisition</td><td>picks the most promising config to try next</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">update</td><td>retrain the guess after each real trial</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Learn a cheap guess of how settings perform, then test the most promising one next.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="search" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Learn a cheap guess of performance, then test the most promising setting next.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

GPyOpt, Optuna's TPE, Google Vizier.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Tunes expensive models (each trial = hours of GPU) in few trials; used for AutoML and even chemistry/hardware design.
:::

:::

:::

:::

:::

# Probability & Statistics

::: {.on-subject}

::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills}

## Foundations

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Random Variables

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">X : outcome → number   (PMF p(x) or PDF f(x))</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">outcome</td><td>a result of a chance experiment</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">X</td><td>maps that outcome to a number</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">p(x) / f(x)</td><td>how likely each value is (discrete / continuous)</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> A number whose value depends on chance, with a rule for how likely each value is.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="dist" data-params="{&quot;kind&quot;: &quot;uniform&quot;}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

A number whose value depends on chance — a die roll, tomorrow's temperature.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

X = sum of two dice, ranging 2–12.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Model inputs, labels, and predictions as random variables; uncertainty estimates; sampling in generative models.
:::

:::

:::

## Distributions

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Bernoulli

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">P(X=1) = p ,   P(X=0) = 1 − p</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">p</td><td>chance of a '1' (success)</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">1 − p</td><td>chance of a '0' (failure)</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">mean = p</td><td>the long-run fraction of 1s</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> One yes/no trial with success probability p.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="dist" data-params="{&quot;kind&quot;: &quot;bernoulli&quot;}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

One coin flip with a possibly-biased coin.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

p = 0.5 for a fair coin.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Binary classification output (spam/not); each pixel of a binarised image; click/no-click modelling.
:::

:::

### Binomial

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">P(X=k) = C(n,k) · pᵏ · (1−p)ⁿ⁻ᵏ</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">C(n,k)</td><td>number of ways to pick which k trials succeed</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">pᵏ</td><td>probability those k succeed</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">(1−p)ⁿ⁻ᵏ</td><td>probability the other n−k fail</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">mean = np</td><td>expected number of successes</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> The chance of getting exactly k successes in n independent yes/no trials.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="dist" data-params="{&quot;kind&quot;: &quot;binomial&quot;}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

How many heads in n coin flips.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

10 flips, p=0.5: expect 5 heads.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Conversion counts in A/B tests; defect counts in QA; correct predictions out of n.
:::

:::

### Poisson

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">P(X=k) = ( λᵏ · e^(−λ) ) / k!</div>
<div class="frm-frac">
  <div class="frm-num">λᵏ · e^(−λ)</div>
  <div class="frm-bar"></div>
  <div class="frm-den">k!</div>
</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Part</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-top">▲ Top<br><span>λᵏ · e^(−λ)</span></td><td>weight for seeing k events when the average rate is λ</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-bot">▼ Bottom<br><span>k!</span></td><td>divide by k-factorial (the count's arrangements)</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> The chance of k rare events in a fixed window when the average count is λ.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="dist" data-params="{&quot;kind&quot;: &quot;poisson&quot;}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

How many rare things happen in a window — calls per hour, typos per page.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

λ = 3 emails/hour.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Arrival rates (web traffic, network packets); count data in NLP; queueing systems.
:::

:::

### Uniform

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">f(x) = 1 / (b − a)   on  [a, b]</div>
<div class="frm-frac">
  <div class="frm-num">1</div>
  <div class="frm-bar"></div>
  <div class="frm-den">b − a</div>
</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Part</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-top">▲ Top<br><span>1</span></td><td>equal weight for every value</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-bot">▼ Bottom<br><span>b − a</span></td><td>spread that weight evenly across the whole range</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Every value in the range is equally likely — total fairness.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="dist" data-params="{&quot;kind&quot;: &quot;uniform&quot;}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Total fairness — every value in the range is just as probable.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

A random float in [0, 1).
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Random weight init, dropout masks, random seeds, Monte-Carlo sampling.
:::

:::

### Gaussian

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">f(x) = ( 1 / √(2πσ²) ) · e^( −(x−μ)² / 2σ² )</div>
<div class="frm-frac">
  <div class="frm-num">e^( −(x−μ)²/2σ² )</div>
  <div class="frm-bar"></div>
  <div class="frm-den">√(2πσ²)</div>
</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Part</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-top">▲ Top<br><span>e^( −(x−μ)²/2σ² )</span></td><td>peaks at the mean μ and falls off as you move away (curve shape)</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-bot">▼ Bottom<br><span>√(2πσ²)</span></td><td>a constant that makes the whole area equal 1</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> The bell curve: most values cluster near the mean μ, fewer appear as you go out by multiples of σ.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="gaussian" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

The classic bell curve — most values near the average, few far out.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Heights, measurement noise; 68% fall within ±1σ.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Weight init, noise models, VAE priors, diffusion noise, Gaussian processes — the most important distribution in ML.
:::

:::

:::

## Moments & Relationships

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Central Limit Theorem

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">mean of many i.i.d. variables  →  Gaussian</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">i.i.d. variables</td><td>many independent samples from the same source</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">their average</td><td>sum them and divide by n</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">→ Gaussian</td><td>that average looks bell-shaped, whatever the source</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Average enough independent random things and the result looks like a bell curve — no matter the source.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="clt" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Average enough random things and the result looks like a bell curve.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

The average of 30+ dice rolls is approximately normal.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Why Gaussian assumptions work so often; the basis of confidence intervals and many statistical tests.
:::

:::

### Expectation

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">E[X] = Σ x · p(x)     ( ∫ x f(x) dx )</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">x</td><td>each possible value</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">p(x)</td><td>its probability (the weight)</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">Σ x·p(x)</td><td>probability-weighted average → the long-run mean</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> The probability-weighted average — what you'd get on average over endless repeats.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="distmark" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

The average value you'd get if you repeated the experiment forever.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Fair die: E[X] = 3.5.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Expected loss is what training minimises; expected reward in RL; expected value drives every risk/decision calc.
:::

:::

### Variance

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">Var(X) = E[ (X − μ)² ]</div>
<div class="frm-frac">
  <div class="frm-num">(X − μ)²</div>
  <div class="frm-bar"></div>
  <div class="frm-den">averaged</div>
</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Part</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-top">▲ Top<br><span>(X − μ)²</span></td><td>squared distance of each value from the mean</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-bot">▼ Bottom<br><span>averaged</span></td><td>take the expected (mean) of those squared distances</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> The average squared distance from the mean — how spread out the values are.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="distmark" data-params="{&quot;variance&quot;: true}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

How spread out the values are around the average — small = consistent, large = erratic.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Fair die variance ≈ 2.92.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Bias–variance tradeoff governs over/underfitting; gradient variance affects stability; risk in finance.
:::

:::

### Covariance

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">Cov(X,Y) = E[ (X − μₓ)(Y − μᵧ) ]</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">(X−μₓ)</td><td>how far X is from its mean</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">(Y−μᵧ)</td><td>how far Y is from its mean</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">their product, averaged</td><td>positive if they move together, negative if oppositely</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Average the product of each pair's deviations — positive means they rise and fall together.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="scatter" data-params="{&quot;rho&quot;: 0.6}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Do two quantities tend to rise and fall together (positive) or oppositely (negative)?
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Height and weight have positive covariance.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

The covariance matrix drives PCA; portfolio risk; feature decorrelation and whitening.
:::

:::

### Correlation

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">ρ = Cov(X,Y) / ( σₓ · σᵧ )</div>
<div class="frm-frac">
  <div class="frm-num">Cov(X,Y)</div>
  <div class="frm-bar"></div>
  <div class="frm-den">σₓ · σᵧ</div>
</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Part</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-top">▲ Top<br><span>Cov(X,Y)</span></td><td>how much X and Y move together</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-bot">▼ Bottom<br><span>σₓ · σᵧ</span></td><td>divide by their spreads → a clean score from −1 to +1</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Covariance scaled by the two spreads, giving a tidy −1…+1 strength of linear relationship.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="scatter" data-params="{&quot;rho&quot;: 0.7}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Covariance normalised to −1…+1; ±1 = perfect line, 0 = no linear link.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

ρ = 0.9 is a strong positive relationship.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Feature selection, detecting redundancy/leakage, exploratory analysis — but correlation ≠ causation.
:::

:::

:::

## Inference

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Sampling

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">draw  x ~ distribution   (inverse-CDF, rejection, MCMC)</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">distribution</td><td>the probability pattern to draw from</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">x ~</td><td>generate values that follow that pattern</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">methods</td><td>inverse-CDF, rejection, Markov-chain Monte Carlo</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Generate example values that follow a chosen probability pattern.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="sampling" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Generating example values that follow a chosen probability pattern.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Sampling from N(0,1) with np.random.randn.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Bootstrapping for uncertainty; mini-batch selection; generating text/images from a model's distribution.
:::

:::

### Confidence Intervals

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">x̄ ± z · ( σ / √n )</div>
<div class="frm-frac">
  <div class="frm-num">σ</div>
  <div class="frm-bar"></div>
  <div class="frm-den">√n</div>
</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Part</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-top">▲ Top<br><span>σ</span></td><td>the spread of the data</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-bot">▼ Bottom<br><span>√n</span></td><td>shrinks with more samples → a tighter interval</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> The sample mean plus/minus a margin that shrinks as you collect more data.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="ci" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

A plausible range for the true value, with a stated level of trust.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

A 95% CI uses z ≈ 1.96.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Reporting metric uncertainty (accuracy ± CI); A/B-test result ranges; scientific reproducibility.
:::

:::

### Hypothesis Testing

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">compare  H₀ (no effect)  vs  H₁ (effect)</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">H₀</td><td>the boring default: nothing is happening</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">H₁</td><td>the claim: there's a real effect</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">test statistic</td><td>reject H₀ if the evidence is strong enough</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> A formal way to decide whether an observed effect is real or just luck.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="hyptest" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

A formal way to decide whether an observed effect is real or just luck.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

t-test, chi-square test, z-test.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Deciding if model B truly beats model A; if a feature matters; if an A/B variant won.
:::

:::

### p-values

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">p = P( data this extreme | H₀ is true )</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">H₀ true</td><td>assume nothing is really going on</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">data this extreme</td><td>chance of seeing a result as surprising as yours</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">small p</td><td>unlikely under H₀ → probably a real effect</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> The chance of seeing your result if nothing were really going on; small means 'probably real'.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="pvalue" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

The chance of seeing your result if nothing were going on — small means a likely real effect.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

p = 0.03 < 0.05 → statistically significant.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Gatekeeper for A/B-test decisions; widely misused (p-hacking) — significance ≠ practical importance.
:::

:::

### A/B Testing

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">randomly split users → A vs B → compare metric</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">random split</td><td>half see A, half see B — fair comparison</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">metric</td><td>the number you care about (conversions, revenue)</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">compare</td><td>test if B's lift is significant, not luck</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Show A to half your users and B to the other half, then measure which performs better — fairly.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="abtest" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Show version A to half your users and B to the other half, then measure which wins.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Testing a new recommendation model against the current one.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

How tech companies ship product and ML changes; needs proper sample size, randomisation, and no peeking.
:::

:::

### MLE

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">θ̂ = argmax  Π p(xᵢ | θ)     (max log-likelihood)</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">p(xᵢ|θ)</td><td>how probable each data point is, given settings θ</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">Π (product)</td><td>multiply across all data points</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">argmax</td><td>pick the θ that makes the observed data most probable</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Choose the parameters that make the data you actually observed as likely as possible.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="mle" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Pick the parameters that make the data you actually observed most probable.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

MLE of a Gaussian's mean is the sample average.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Training many models IS maximum likelihood — minimising cross-entropy = maximising label likelihood.
:::

:::

### MAP

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">θ̂ = argmax  p(x | θ) · p(θ)</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Piece</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">p(x|θ)</td><td>likelihood: how well θ explains the data (as in MLE)</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">p(θ)</td><td>prior: your belief about reasonable θ before seeing data</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-sym">argmax of product</td><td>balance the two → like MLE plus regularisation</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Like MLE, but also weigh in prior beliefs about which parameters are reasonable.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="map" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Like MLE, but you also bring in prior beliefs about reasonable parameter values.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

A Gaussian prior on weights ⟺ L2 regularisation.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Explains why weight decay works; injects domain knowledge; stabilises estimates with little data.
:::

:::

### Bayesian Statistics

::: {.onenote-page}

::: {.on-card .on-math}
<div class="on-label">🧮 The Formula — read as a table</div>

```{=html}
<div class="frm-wrap">
<div class="frm-formula">p(θ | x) = ( p(x | θ) · p(θ) ) / p(x)</div>
<div class="frm-frac">
  <div class="frm-num">p(x | θ) · p(θ)</div>
  <div class="frm-bar"></div>
  <div class="frm-den">p(x)</div>
</div>
<table class="frm-table"><thead><tr><th>Part</th><th>In plain words</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td class="frm-top">▲ Top<br><span>p(x | θ) · p(θ)</span></td><td>likelihood × prior — evidence combined with belief</td></tr>
<tr><td class="frm-bot">▼ Bottom<br><span>p(x)</span></td><td>a normaliser so the posterior sums/integrates to 1</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="frm-reads"><strong>📖 Reads as:</strong> Update beliefs with data: posterior ∝ likelihood × prior; keep a full distribution, not one number.</div>
</div>
```

::: {.viz-wrap}
**🎮 Try it — interactive demo**

<div class="ml-viz" data-viz="bayes" data-params="{}"></div>

:::
:::

::: {.on-card .on-simple}
<div class="on-label">💡 Simplified</div>

Start with a belief, see evidence, revise it — and keep a full distribution, not just one number.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-ex}
<div class="on-label">📘 Examples & References</div>

Bayesian A/B testing; Bayesian neural networks.
:::

::: {.on-card .on-real}
<div class="on-label">⚙️ Real-World Values & Applications</div>

Uncertainty-aware predictions (medicine, autonomy); Bayesian optimisation for tuning; naive-Bayes spam filters.
:::

:::

:::

:::

:::
